edges.target: E6/E7 Inactivation of p53 and pRB

edges.source: E6/E7 Inactivation of p53 and pRB

nodes.description: HPV E6 causes degradation of p53, impairing the cellular stress response, while HPV E7 impairs the activity of the retinoblastoma protein (pRB), resulting in continuous cell cycle propagation. Combined loss of p53 and pRB function removes the principal checkpoints that normally restrain proliferation of damaged cells.
nodes.id: E6/E7 Inactivation of p53 and pRB

evidence.reference_title: Novel therapeutic strategies for targeting E6 and E7 oncoproteins in cervical cancer
evidence.snippet: The E6 protein causes degradation of p53 leading to impaired cellular stress response. In contrast, the E7 protein impairs the activity of retinoblastoma protein (pRb) resulting in continuous cell cycle propagation.

nodes.description: Continuous cell-cycle propagation driven by HPV E6/E7 activity permits accumulation of DNA damage and progression of the affected squamous epithelium through cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN1 -> CIN2 -> CIN3) before invasion.

evidence.reference_title: Novel therapeutic strategies for targeting E6 and E7 oncoproteins in cervical cancer
evidence.snippet: The E7 protein impairs the activity of retinoblastoma protein (pRb) resulting in continuous cell cycle propagation.

nodes.description: Persistent infection of the cervical squamous epithelium by high-risk HPV (most commonly HPV-16) is the main aetiological event in cervical squamous cell carcinoma. Persistent infection commits infected cells to a transforming viral cycle in which the E6 and E7 oncoproteins are continuously expressed.

evidence.reference_title: Novel therapeutic strategies for targeting E6 and E7 oncoproteins in cervical cancer
evidence.snippet: The main aetiological factor for developing cervical cancer is the persistent infection of Human papillomavirus (HPV).

nodes.description: Invasive cervical squamous cell carcinoma is defined by breach of the basement membrane and invasion of the cervical stroma by malignant squamous cells, enabling lymphovascular spread and distant metastasis.

evidence.reference_title: The Polish Society of Gynecological Oncology Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Cervical Cancer (v2024.0)
evidence.snippet: Stromal invasion and lymphovascular space involvement (LVSI) from pretreatment biopsy identify candidates for surgery, particularly for simple hysterectomy.
